title: Louie Moves Uptown

jokes.text: Checks? Ever since my dad died and my brother took charge of my estate, I've been getting checks, lots of checks.
jokes.setup: Alex assumes Iggy is complaining about bills like everyone else
jokes.punchline: Iggy reveals he's rich and getting money constantly
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration - only Iggy would be so oblivious about wealth. Sets up the entire episode's premise.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What's wrong, Iggy? You worried about bills like the rest of us?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Bills? No, no. I'm rich. I've got money coming in constantly.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You're rich?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Sure. Every day more money arrives. It never stops.

jokes.text: When you cash them, what have you got? Money. Money can be lost, stolen or wasted and you feel terrible. This way, if anything happens to them, what have I lost? Checks.
jokes.setup: Alex asks the obvious question about why not cash checks
jokes.punchline: Iggy's backwards logic about checks being safer than money
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic that's perfectly in character. The circular reasoning is both illogical and somehow compelling.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Why don't you just cash the checks?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Cash them? Are you crazy? Checks are much safer than money.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Safer? How are checks safer than money?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Well, if someone steals a check, they can't spend it. But if someone steals money, they can spend it right away.

jokes.text: I've heard about business managers running off to Mexico with their clients' money. And if you did that, I'd miss you like crazy.
jokes.setup: Iggy expresses concern about business managers stealing money
jokes.punchline: He's more worried about missing Louie than losing the money
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Iggy's priorities and naivety about money vs. relationships.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: You know what worries me most about business managers stealing money?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: I'd miss you more than the money.

jokes.text: You'll need your driver's license. Oh, you need two IDs? Yeah. Got it!
jokes.setup: Alex explains Iggy needs his driver's license as second ID
jokes.punchline: Iggy's overly enthusiastic response to needing two IDs
jokes.explanation: Simple character beat showing Iggy's enthusiasm for mundane bureaucracy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You need two forms of ID.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Two?! That's great! I'll get my license and my library card!

jokes.text: I'm going to be a little bit late. I love to touch her all over when she's like this.
jokes.setup: Latka storms out angrily
jokes.punchline: Tony's inappropriate sexual comment about his angry wife
jokes.explanation: Classic Tony inappropriate overshare - perfectly in character for the boxer's crude sensibilities.

jokes.text: The wife of the guy I like most in this garage just said I'm not welcome in their home anymore.
jokes.setup: Louie explains why he's upset
jokes.punchline: Revealing that Tony is his favorite person despite never showing it
jokes.explanation: Rare moment of Louie vulnerability that reveals depth beneath his cruelty.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what? Tony is my favorite person in this whole cab company. Yeah, that's right. My favorite.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But do I go around telling him that? Do I give him compliments? Do I treat him nice? No. Because that's not how I operate.

jokes.text: It's cheap, but it's a sewer. I don't know why I stay. Because it's you?
jokes.setup: Louie wonders aloud why he stays in his awful apartment
jokes.punchline: Elaine's brutal but accurate assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing. Elaine's deadpan delivery of the obvious truth is devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Why do I stay in this awful apartment? The rent's too high, the plumbing doesn't work, there's a stain on the ceiling I can't identify...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Because you're a bitter, lonely man with no friends and nowhere else to go.

jokes.text: I've always wanted a really great apartment. And, you know, something plush with the pipes inside the walls.
jokes.setup: Louie describes his dream apartment
jokes.punchline: His idea of luxury is basic plumbing
jokes.explanation: Shows how low Louie's standards are - indoor plumbing is aspirational luxury to him.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what my dream apartment is? A place where the toilet flushes on the first try, the shower has hot water, and the radiator doesn't sound like a dying animal at three in the morning.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's luxury to me.

jokes.text: Louie! Ignore it! Ignore it! Ignore it! That was the old Louie saying good-bye.
jokes.setup: The phone rings and Louie starts to respond rudely
jokes.punchline: He catches himself and claims it's the 'old Louie'
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Louie's attempt at self-improvement and awareness of his bad habits.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, what do ya want?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm sorry, I apologize. That was the old Louie. The new Louie is much more pleasant.

jokes.text: Look, look, look, it makes those little ice cubes with the hole in it. Whoo!
jokes.setup: Louie discovers an ice maker in the fancy apartment
jokes.punchline: His childlike wonder at hollow ice cubes
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's amazement at basic modern conveniences - endearing character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What is this? How does it work? Look at these ice cubes... they're hollow! They're hollow!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: In all my years, I never seen nothing like this. Hollow ice cubes. This is fancy. This is real fancy.

jokes.text: I read somewhere where this guy in Jersey claims that he got sterilized by one of these, but I figure he must have been cooking in some weird position.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions microwave sterilization urban legend
jokes.punchline: His bizarre theory about 'cooking position' causing it
jokes.explanation: Absurd visual image and perfectly wrong understanding of how microwaves work.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what I heard? If you put a wedding ring in the microwave, it sterilizes you. That's why so many married guys can't have kids.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It's the cooking position. You're standing right there next to it while it's running. The radiation, it goes right through ya.

jokes.text: We wanted a place like this all our lives. That long, huh?
jokes.setup: Young couple says they've wanted this apartment all their lives
jokes.punchline: Louie's sarcastic comment about their youth
jokes.explanation: Quick, cutting comeback that deflates their enthusiasm with logic.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: All their lives? They're twelve years old!

jokes.text: Scott doesn't want to have children right away. Go stand by the microwave.
jokes.setup: Wife mentions husband doesn't want children yet
jokes.punchline: Louie suggests using the microwave for sterilization
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the microwave sterilization joke, showing Louie's crude problem-solving.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sheffield
dialogue.line: My husband doesn't want children yet.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, tell him to put his shorts in the microwave.

jokes.text: $95,000... I'll take it!
jokes.setup: Young couple is shocked by the $95,000 price and leaves
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately commits to the huge sum
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's impulsiveness and how his newfound wealth changes his perspective.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: $95,000? I'll take it!

jokes.text: Okay, but if I'm gonna pay that kind of money, I want a cab with a heater.
jokes.setup: Louie asks Iggy for $48,000 for a cab
jokes.punchline: Iggy's only condition is wanting a heater
jokes.explanation: Perfect Iggy moment - completely missing the absurdity of the price, focusing on minor comfort.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I need $48,000 for a cab.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: I'll lend it to you on one condition.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: The cab's gotta have a heater.

jokes.text: You called me 'Jim.' This marks a turning point in our relationship. I want to remember this day. The red-letter day when my boss and buddy finally called...
jokes.setup: Louie desperately asks Iggy for money
jokes.punchline: Iggy is more excited about being called by his first name
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing Iggy values personal connection over money, contrasts beautifully with Louie's desperation.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Iggy, I need to borrow some money. Please, I'm desperate.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: You called me Iggy! You know my first name! Nobody ever calls me by my first name!

jokes.text: I know they say that money can't buy love. But if you give me these for 48 big ones, it'll be Valentine's Day forever.
jokes.setup: Louie tries to sweet-talk Iggy into the loan
jokes.punchline: His crude attempt at romantic persuasion for money
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's manipulative nature and his clumsy attempt at charm.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Iggy, baby, you know I think you're the most beautiful woman in this garage.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Louie, what do you want?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Can't a man just appreciate a woman's... assets?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: How much do you need to borrow?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Just five hundred bucks, sweetheart. What do you say? For old times' sake?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: The answer is no.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Come on, gorgeous. Don't break my heart.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Your heart broke a long time ago, Louie. Your wallet's about to join it.

jokes.text: Three percent. 12.75. Okeydoke.
jokes.setup: Louie tries to lowball the interest rate at 3%
jokes.punchline: Iggy immediately accepts Alex's much higher rate without caring
jokes.explanation: Shows both Louie's greed and Iggy's complete indifference to money.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'll give you 3% interest.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: 3%? That's ridiculous. I want 12%.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Done. I'll take it.

jokes.text: When I applied to Harvard, they told me I'd never get past their stodgy admissions board. But I did. All you've got to do is dress nicely, shake hands firmly, and have your dad finance a gymnasium.
jokes.setup: Iggy offers advice about getting past snobbish boards
jokes.punchline: Reveals his acceptance was bought, not earned
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with great build-up to the punchline about privilege and corruption.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: You gotta tell them you're from a working-class background, that you pulled yourself up by your bootstraps. They eat that stuff up.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Of course, in my case, my father bought my way in. But they don't need to know that.

jokes.text: I think they should be condemned.
jokes.setup: Louie asks if his fingernails should be trimmed
jokes.punchline: Alex suggests they need condemnation instead
jokes.explanation: Escalates from simple grooming advice to suggesting his nails are a health hazard.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Should I get my fingernails trimmed?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Trimmed? They need condemnation.

jokes.text: Well, then it's our job to put him at his ease.
jokes.setup: Alex explains Louie is self-conscious about his appearance
jokes.punchline: Iggy's well-meaning but completely wrong assessment
jokes.explanation: Shows Iggy's innocent misunderstanding of the situation and desire to help.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Louie's a good-looking guy. I mean, sure, he's got that thing going on with his face, but that just gives him character. Plus, he's got a great personality. Women love that.

jokes.text: When you go to a clothing store, you got one arm in the suit and the salesman runs off to help another guy who looks like a better prospect.
jokes.setup: Continuing the examples of discrimination
jokes.punchline: Vivid image of being literally abandoned mid-transaction
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the discrimination theme with a specific, humiliating scenario.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, I've seen discrimination in this city. But nothing compares to what happened to me last week at the bank.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm standing at the teller window, trying to cash a check. The teller takes one look at me, turns around, and just walks away.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Doesn't say a word. Doesn't come back. I'm standing there with my check in the air like an idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Ten minutes later, the manager comes over and says, 'We're closing.' It's 2 PM on a Tuesday.

jokes.text: Or when you walk into a bank to open an account and a security guard undoes the flap on his holster.
jokes.setup: Final example in the discrimination series
jokes.punchline: Being treated as a criminal threat just for existing
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny escalation that gets to the heart of discrimination - being seen as dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Being treated as a criminal threat just for existing

jokes.text: What do you mean, 'give you a look'? I don't know. Perhaps something like this?
jokes.setup: Alex asks what kind of look Louie wants if he says something stupid
jokes.punchline: Iggy demonstrates with an exaggerated facial expression
jokes.explanation: Visual gag that shows Iggy's misunderstanding of the situation and eagerness to help.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What kind of look do you want me to give you if you say something stupid?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: *makes exaggerated facial expression*

jokes.text: What do you mean, 'give you a look'? I don't know. Perhaps something like this? Iggy, will you cut it out.
jokes.setup: Louie asks Alex to give him a warning look if he says something stupid
jokes.punchline: Jim demonstrates with an exaggerated facial expression
jokes.explanation: Visual gag with Jim's characteristic over-the-top physical comedy

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Give me a warning look if I say something stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'll give you a warning look.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: [demonstrates with an exaggerated facial expression]

jokes.text: ♪ Schlemiel, Schlimazel, Hasenpfeffer Incorporated ♪
jokes.setup: Co-op board asks about Elaine's TV show
jokes.punchline: She sings the Laverne & Shirley theme song
jokes.explanation: Meta-reference to actress Marla Henner's previous role, perfectly integrated into character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Schlemiel! Schlimazel! Hasenpfeffer Incorporated!

jokes.text: They pay you all that money to be a chicken? I just don't act like a chicken. It's based on reality.
jokes.setup: Board member is incredulous about being paid to act like a chicken
jokes.punchline: Elaine insists it's 'based on reality'
jokes.explanation: Absurd attempt to justify ridiculous sitcom premise as realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Board Member
dialogue.line: You're paying me to act like a chicken?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's based on reality.

jokes.text: If I ding like a bell, would you do the chicken? Oh, please, lady. Oh, please, you do want to live in this building, don't you?
jokes.setup: Board member asks Elaine to demonstrate the chicken act
jokes.punchline: Her desperate agreement when pressured about wanting the apartment
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable moment showing how desperation leads to humiliation.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yes! Yes, I want the apartment! I'll do the chicken!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'll do it right now! Watch me!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Bawk bawk bawk bawk!

jokes.text: We wouldn't allow an actor to live here even if he were English.
jokes.setup: Board explains their prejudice against show business people
jokes.punchline: Even English actors (supposedly more respectable) are unwelcome
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about class prejudices and anglophilia in American culture.

dialogue.speaker: Board Member
dialogue.line: We have nothing against show business people, you understand. It's just that they're all cocaine addicts, they sleep with each other's wives, and they're basically unemployable.

dialogue.speaker: Board Member
dialogue.line: Even English actors — and they're supposedly more respectable — we won't let them in the building.

jokes.text: Look, I don't know what you do for a living, but if they accept you, please don't let me know.
jokes.setup: Elaine encounters Louie after her rejection
jokes.punchline: Her assumption that anyone they'd accept must be terrible
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing her frustration and logical conclusion about the board's standards.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So you rejected me, huh? What, am I not good enough for this place?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's right, you're not.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, who do you hire? What kind of person would be good enough for you?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: We got standards here.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? So who works here?

jokes.text: Well, uh, I'm-I'm a supervisor in the transportation field. Mr. De Palma is a taxi dispatcher.
jokes.setup: Louie tries to make his job sound impressive
jokes.punchline: Alex immediately deflates his pretension with the truth
jokes.explanation: Classic deflation of pretension - Alex's honesty undercuts Louie's attempt at respectability.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm a dispatcher. I coordinate the entire fleet, manage communications, handle logistics...

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You yell at drivers and eat donuts.

jokes.text: Oh, and what makes you assume that I'm Jewish? Point well made, sir. We shouldn't jump to conclusions. Perhaps someone in his family was a pelican.
jokes.setup: Alex challenges the anti-Semitic assumption
jokes.punchline: Board member's bizarre pelican theory about his appearance
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd response that reveals both casual bigotry and complete lack of logic.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You're saying I look like a pelican?

dialogue.speaker: Board Member
dialogue.line: Well, not exactly. But you have to admit, there's a certain resemblance around the nose and the way you carry yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Board Member
dialogue.line: Is it? Have you ever seen a pelican and a Jewish man in the same room together?

jokes.text: Once a year the British ambassador comes over for a spot of sherry. Is that all right with you?
jokes.setup: Board asks about what kind of friends Louie has
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic claim about hosting the British ambassador
jokes.explanation: Louie's sarcastic response that plays into their apparent anglophilia.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What do you mean, what kind of friends do I have? I got plenty of friends. Why, just last week I was hosting the British ambassador at my place.

jokes.text: You're fired. You are no longer my accountant.
jokes.setup: Alex defends Louie and storms out in moral outrage
jokes.punchline: Louie pretends Alex was just his accountant to save face
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - shows his selfishness and willingness to betray Alex's loyalty.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: He wasn't defending me. He was my accountant. That was my accountant!

jokes.text: I think you've shown us what we need to know. Welcome to the building.
jokes.setup: Louie throws Alex under the bus to impress the board
jokes.punchline: His betrayal of a friend is exactly what they want to see
jokes.explanation: Dark irony - his worst character trait is what gets him accepted. Brilliant commentary on class.

dialogue.speaker: Board Member
dialogue.line: We like the way you throw your people under the bus. That's the kind of ruthlessness we need in management.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, I learned from the best.

jokes.text: Are you gonna go through with this? So that you can have the privilege of living here with these-these posturing chowderheads?
jokes.setup: Alex challenges Louie about accepting the apartment
jokes.punchline: His creative insult 'posturing chowderheads'
jokes.explanation: Creative insult that captures both their pretension and emptiness.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know what you are, Louie? You're nothing but a couple of posturing chowderheads.

jokes.text: Cartwheels, all the way home.
jokes.setup: Board member asks Alex what he's going to do about them accepting Louie
jokes.punchline: Louie's gleeful response about doing cartwheels
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's childish glee at his victory and complete lack of shame.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna do cartwheels!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna do cartwheels all the way down the stairs!

tmdb_overview: Louie decides to buy a luxury co-op apartment with some help on the down payment from the newly wealthy Jim. Unfortunately, Louie first has to convince a snooty co-op board that he is right for their building (as he comes in, they are rejecting actress Penny Marshall because they don't approve of the actress lifestyle).  Bringing Alex for support, Louie has his work cut out for him when he sees that the board is bigoted. Alex goes on a tirade against their discriminatory process, and the board accepts Louie just to spite Alex.
insight: Louie's apartment hunt yields steady laughs but never breaks the ensemble's usual rhythm.
